For Version A, the mean interaction time is 45 seconds with a standard deviation of 5 seconds, and for Version B, the mean is 40 seconds with a standard deviation of 3 seconds. Assuming the interaction times are independent and the sample sizes are large enough, we can use the Central Limit Theorem to approximate the distribution of the sample means. The standard deviation of the sample mean for Version A is $ \frac{5}{\sqrt{30}} \approx 0.9129 $, and for Version B, it is $ \frac{3}{\sqrt{30}} \approx 0.5477 $. The difference in means is $ 45 - 40 = 5 $ seconds. Using the standard normal distribution, the probability that the average interaction time for Version B is less than that of Version A can be calculated as follows:

$$
P(\bar{X}_B < \bar{X}_A) = P\left( \frac{\bar{X}_B - \bar{X}_A}{\sqrt{\frac{\sigma_B^2}{n} + \frac{\sigma_A^2}{n}}} < \frac{5}{\sqrt{\frac{9}{30} + \frac{25}{30}}} \right)
$$

$$
= P\left( Z < \frac{5}{\sqrt{\frac{34}{30}}} \right) = P(Z < \frac{5}{\sqrt{1.1333}}) \approx P(Z < 4.36)
$$

This probability is extremely high, nearly 1, since the z-score is far to the right of the standard normal distribution. Therefore, the probability that the average interaction time for a random sample of 30 users in Version B is less than that of Version A is approximately 0.9999.
